data.title: Ghost Murmur Rescue Puts Quantum Sensing At Core Of Lockheed Martin Story
data.description:
• Lockheed Martin's classified "Ghost Murmur" quantum sensing system supported a combat rescue mission in Iran, enabling detection of a downed U.S. airman's heartbeat from long distance.
  • The operation highlights a new class of military sensing capability for search and rescue, survivability, and special operations that differs from previously covered missile and space contracts.
  • The company, traded as NYSE:LMT, is now associated with a field deployment of quantum sensing technology that had not been publicly linked to live combat missions before this event.

For investors tracking NYSE:LMT, this development adds a new dimension to a story that has recently focused on major missile programs and the Artemis II mission. The stock last closed at $611.1, with a 22.9% return year to date and a 31.6% return over the past year.

The Ghost Murmur deployment introduces an additional angle for thinking about Lockheed Martin's role in next generation defense sensing, especially for high risk missions where rapid location of personnel is critical. Investors may want to watch how often this class of technology appears in future defense priorities and whether similar capabilities are referenced in upcoming programs or customer discussions.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Lockheed Martin by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Lockheed Martin.

4 things going right for Lockheed Martin that this headline doesn't cover.

The Ghost Murmur rescue adds a different layer to the Lockheed Martin story than missile orders or the Artemis II win. It shows the company applying quantum sensing to a live combat search-and-rescue problem, which points to a business model that stretches beyond hardware platforms into sensor architectures, signal processing and software heavy mission systems. For investors, that raises questions about how much of Lockheed Martin’s long term growth could come from classified, high margin technologies that sit inside broader programs rather than from headline platforms alone. It also sharpens the competitive picture against peers like Northrop Grumman, RTX and BAE Systems that are also targeting advanced sensing and electronic warfare. The key issue is execution, including how reliably Ghost Murmur type capabilities can be fielded, integrated with existing command-and-control networks and supported at scale across services and allied customers.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Lockheed Martin Narrative

  • Ghost Murmur aligns with the narrative that emphasizes next generation technologies, R&D investment and advanced defense capabilities as factors in potential future contracts and an expanding addressable market.
  • The classified, mission critical nature of the system also highlights execution and cost risk on complex programs, which echoes concerns in the narrative about program charges and contract discipline.
  • Quantum sensing for personnel recovery is not clearly broken out in existing themes such as munitions or major platforms, so investors may not yet have fully incorporated this type of capability into their view of Lockheed Martin’s long term mix.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Lockheed Martin to help decide what it is worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Quantum sensing programs are complex, so cost overruns, schedule slips or technical setbacks could pressure margins on classified contracts.
  • ⚠️ A high level of debt means any delay in converting advanced technology into dependable cash flows can tighten financial flexibility if spending priorities change.
  • 🎁 Successful deployment of Ghost Murmur would reinforce Lockheed Martin’s positioning in high end defense technology, which may support the view that it is trading at good value relative to peers and industry.
  • 🎁 The company already has 4 identified rewards, including trading at 7.2% below one fair value estimate, earnings growth expectations and a 2.26% dividend, so additional capabilities can add another angle to the investment case.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, investors can watch for any follow on references to quantum sensing, personnel recovery or advanced ISR in contract announcements and defense budget priorities, especially compared with offerings from Northrop Grumman and RTX. Updates on Lockheed Martin’s R&D and venture investing focus will also matter, because they indicate how quickly concepts like Ghost Murmur might scale into repeatable programs. Given the identified single company risk around debt and the set of 4 rewards, it may be worth tracking how management talks about balancing investment in classified technologies with cash generation and capital returns.

data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Steel Dynamics (STLD) After A 68% One Year Rally?
data.description:
• If you are asking whether Steel Dynamics at US$194.11 still offers value or is pricing in too much optimism, the starting point is to look closely at how its current share price lines up with different valuation checks.
  • The stock has returned 3.5% over the past 7 days, 11.4% over 30 days, 10.3% year to date, and 67.8% over 1 year, which naturally raises questions about what is already reflected in the price.
  • Recent coverage has focused on Steel Dynamics as a key name in US-listed steel producers, with attention on how its position in the sector relates to industrial activity and capital spending. Commentary around the stock often highlights how investors are weighing sector specific factors against broader materials sentiment when interpreting these returns.
  • On Simply Wall St's 6 point valuation framework, Steel Dynamics records a value score of 3 out of 6. The next sections will break down what that means across different methods and will finish with a broader way to think about value that goes beyond a single score.

Find out why Steel Dynamics's 67.8% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Steel Dynamics Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes expected future cash flows, then discounts them back to today to estimate what the business might be worth right now. It is essentially a way of translating future cash generation into a single present value figure.

For Steel Dynamics, Simply Wall St applies a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model using cash flow projections expressed in US$. The latest twelve month free cash flow is reported as a loss of about $42.3 million, so the model leans heavily on expected future free cash flow rather than recent figures. Analyst and extrapolated estimates suggest free cash flow reaching about $3.3 billion in 2035, with intermediate projections such as $2.0 billion in 2026 and $2.6 billion in 2028, all discounted back to today using the DCF framework.

On this basis, the estimated intrinsic value comes out at roughly $310.17 per share, compared with the current share price of about $194.11. That implies a 37.4% discount, which indicates that Steel Dynamics is trading below this model-based estimate of fair value.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Steel Dynamics is undervalued by 37.4%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 60 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Steel Dynamics.

Approach 2: Steel Dynamics Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful guide because it links what you pay for the stock to the earnings the business is currently generating. The higher the expected earnings growth and the lower the perceived risk, the more investors are often willing to pay in terms of a higher P/E multiple, while slower growth or higher risk usually supports a lower, more conservative P/E.

Steel Dynamics currently trades on a P/E of 23.7x. This sits above the peer group average of 20.2x and also above the broader Metals and Mining industry average of 22.9x. This suggests the market is applying a somewhat richer earnings multiple than these simple benchmarks. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Steel Dynamics is 27.4x, which is the P/E level implied by its model after factoring in elements such as earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market size and company specific risks.

This Fair Ratio can be more informative than a straight peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for those underlying differences rather than assuming all companies deserve the same multiple. Since the Fair Ratio of 27.4x is above the current P/E of 23.7x, this approach suggests the shares may be trading below that model based earnings multiple.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Why BTIG’s Neutral View and Recent Weakness Matter for General Mills Stock

BTIG’s new Neutral rating on General Mills (GIS), coming shortly after earnings that missed expectations but left guidance unchanged, lands at a time when the stock is trading near multi year lows.

This combination of cautious commentary, pressured growth fundamentals, and a leveraged balance sheet is pushing investors to reassess what they are paying for the company’s brands, cash flows, and income profile today.

This suggests momentum has been fading as recent earnings, a leveraged balance sheet, and new euro denominated subordinated bond issuance keep risk perceptions elevated.
